hitler\\bio.json:
{
  "id": "hitler-bio",
  "subject": "Adolf Hitler",
  "born": "20 April 1889",
  "birthplace": "Braunau am Inn, Austria-Hungary",
  "died": "30 April 1945",
  "place_of_death": "Führerbunker, Berlin",
  "full_name": "Adolf Hitler",
  "titles": ["Chancellor of Germany (1933–1945)", "Führer und Reichskanzler (1934–1945)", "Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces"],
  "biography": "Adolf Hitler was the leader of the National Socialist German Workers' Party (NSDAP) and dictator of Germany from 1933 to 1945. Born in Austria, he served as a corporal in the German Army during World War I and received the Iron Cross First Class. Embittered by Germany's defeat and the subsequent Versailles Treaty, he became politically active in Munich, joining the fledgling NSDAP and transforming it from a minor extremist group into a mass movement.\n\nAfter the failed Beer Hall Putsch of 1923, Hitler pursued the 'legal path' to power. Exploiting the Great Depression and the political instability of the Weimar Republic, he was appointed Chancellor on 30 January 1933. Within months he had consolidated dictatorial power through the Enabling Act and established the Third Reich.\n\nHis regime pursued radical policies of German expansion, racial antisemitism, and the systematic murder of European Jews in the Holocaust. His decision to launch the Second World War and to pursue a war of extermination against the Soviet Union led ultimately to Germany's total defeat. He committed suicide in the Führerbunker in Berlin on 30 April 1945 as Soviet forces fought through the city.",
  "early_life": {
    "childhood": "Born to Alois Hitler, a customs official, and Klara Pölzl. His father died in 1903; his mother in 1907, a loss that affected him deeply.",
    "vienna": "Applied twice to the Vienna Academy of Fine Arts; rejected both times. Spent formative years in Vienna (1908–1913) in poverty, developing his antisemitic worldview.",
    "ww1": "Volunteered for the German Army in 1914; served as a dispatch runner with the 16th Bavarian Reserve Infantry Regiment; wounded twice; awarded Iron Cross First and Second Class."
  }
}
overview.json:
{
  "name": "Nationalsozialistische Deutsche Arbeiterpartei (NSDAP)",
  "name_english": "National Socialist German Workers' Party",
  "abbreviation": "NSDAP",
  "founded": "24 February 1920",
  "dissolved": "10 October 1945",
  "leader": "Adolf Hitler (1921–1945)",
  "ideology": "National Socialism, Fascism, Antisemitism, Ultranationalism",
  "peakMembership": "8,500,000 (1945)",
  "description": "The NSDAP was the far-right political party that governed Germany from 1933 to 1945 under Adolf Hitler. Founded in Munich in 1919 as the German Workers' Party (DAP), it was reorganised and renamed by Hitler in 1920. The party's ideology — National Socialism — combined extreme German nationalism with racial antisemitism, anticommunism, and a rejection of parliamentary democracy.\n\nThe party rose to power during the political and economic instability of the Weimar Republic. Hitler was appointed Chancellor on 30 January 1933 and the party consolidated total control of Germany through the Enabling Act of March 1933, transforming the republic into a single-party totalitarian state.\n\nUnder NSDAP rule, Germany undertook massive rearmament, territorial expansion, and ultimately launched the Second World War in 1939. The party's racial ideology was implemented through increasingly radical persecution of Jews and other groups, culminating in the Holocaust — the systematic murder of approximately six million European Jews.",
  "headquarters": "Munich, Bavaria",
  "youth_wings": ["Hitler Youth (Hitlerjugend)", "League of German Girls (BDM)"],
  "paramilitary": ["Sturmabteilung (SA)", "Schutzstaffel (SS)"],
  "newspaper": "Völkischer Beobachter",
  "anthem": "Horst-Wessel-Lied",
  "note": "This page documents the NSDAP for educational and historical research purposes only. The ideology documented here is presented objectively as history and is not endorsed."
}
party\\dissolution.json:
{
  "title": "Dissolution of the NSDAP",
  "description": "The end of the National Socialist party following Germany's defeat.",
  "content": "The NSDAP was formally banned by the Allied Control Council Law No. 2 on 10 October 1945. All party organisations, affiliated bodies, and successor organisations were simultaneously banned. Nazi symbols, insignia, and propaganda were prohibited.

The Nuremberg Laws and all racially discriminatory legislation were annulled. The de-Nazification process (Entnazifizierung) was initiated across all four Allied occupation zones to remove former Nazis from positions of influence in public life, the judiciary, education, and business.

Approximately 3.5 million Germans faced de-Nazification proceedings; of these, only 1,667 were classified as 'major offenders' and 22,000 as 'offenders'. Critics argued that de-Nazification was inconsistently applied and that many former Nazis returned to prominent positions in West German society, particularly in the judiciary.

Membership in or promotion of National Socialism has remained illegal in Germany, Austria, and several other countries ever since."
}
party\\formation.json:
{
  "title": "Formation of the NSDAP",
  "description": "The origins and early development of the National Socialist German Workers' Party.",
  "content": "The NSDAP grew out of the Deutsche Arbeiterpartei (DAP), founded by Anton Drexler and Karl Harrer in Munich on 5 January 1919. The party combined German nationalism, antisemitism, and a vague anti-capitalist rhetoric appealing to those who felt cheated by Germany's defeat in World War I.

Adolf Hitler joined as member No. 555 (the numbering began at 500 to suggest greater membership) in September 1919. His oratorical ability transformed the party; by 1921 he had assumed leadership and renamed it the NSDAP, adopting the swastika as its symbol.

The 25-Point Programme announced on 24 February 1920 defined the party's ideology: rejection of Versailles, German expansion, racial purity, antisemitism, and a nationalist economic programme. The programme was declared 'unalterable' — though in practice Hitler discarded inconvenient points as circumstances demanded.

After the failed Beer Hall Putsch of November 1923, the party was refounded in February 1925 with Hitler committing to the legal path to power through elections."
}
party\\leadership.json:
{
  "title": "NSDAP Leadership",
  "description": "The principal leaders of the National Socialist regime, their roles, and their fates.",
  "leaders": [
    { "name": "Adolf Hitler", "role": "Führer und Reichskanzler", "fate": "Suicide, 30 April 1945, Berlin" },
    { "name": "Hermann Göring", "role": "Reichsmarschall; Head of Luftwaffe; designated successor until 1945", "fate": "Sentenced to death at Nuremberg; suicide by cyanide, 15 October 1946" },
    { "name": "Heinrich Himmler", "role": "Reichsführer-SS; Head of Gestapo and SS; Interior Minister", "fate": "Suicide by cyanide after capture, 23 May 1945" },
    { "name": "Joseph Goebbels", "role": "Reich Minister of Propaganda; Reich Chancellor (1 day)", "fate": "Suicide with wife Magda (who killed their 6 children), 1 May 1945, Berlin" },
    { "name": "Martin Bormann", "role": "Head of the Party Chancellery; Hitler's personal secretary", "fate": "Died attempting to escape Berlin, 1–2 May 1945 (confirmed 1998 by DNA)" },
    { "name": "Rudolf Hess", "role": "Deputy Führer until 1941; flew solo to Scotland", "fate": "Life sentence at Nuremberg; suicide in Spandau Prison, 17 August 1987, age 93" },
    { "name": "Joachim von Ribbentrop", "role": "Foreign Minister", "fate": "Hanged at Nuremberg, 16 October 1946" },
    { "name": "Albert Speer", "role": "Reich Minister for Armaments; Hitler's architect", "fate": "20 years in Spandau; released 1966; died 1981" },
    { "name": "Karl Dönitz", "role": "Commander U-boats; then Commander Kriegsmarine; Hitler's successor as Reich President", "fate": "10 years imprisonment at Nuremberg; released 1956; died 1980" }
  ]
}
